data.title: Lululemon Faces Further Pain Ahead as Casual Offerings Grow Stale — Analysis
data.description:
(16:37 GMT) *Lululemon Could Face Further Guidance Cuts Ahead, Analysts Say
(16:37 GMT) *Lululemon Shares Likely to Remain Under Pressure Amid Product Struggles in U.S., Analysts Say
(16:37 GMT) Lululemon Faces Further Pain Ahead as Casual Offerings Grow Stale — Analysis

By Kelly Cloonan

Some of Lululemon's core products aren't generating the same buzz they used to in an increasingly crowded market for casual athleticwear, a dynamic that could keep the company's performance under pressure for some time.

The brand known for its yoga pants and scuba sweatshirts spooked investors on Thursday with a cut to sales and profit guidance for the year after second-quarter results fell short of expectations, with underperformance in its U.S. business and a bigger hit from tariffs. Shares dropped 19% Friday and are down about 56% this year.

Chief Executive Calvin McDonald offered a stark critique of some of the problems behind the company's recent subpar results, calling its loungewear offerings "stale" and citing intensifying competition in recent years.

Some analysts noted that Lululemon's tone has shifted, with executives more overtly acknowledging the competitive threats to its perch in the activewear dominion as its efforts to spur demand with new colors fell flat. While the company proposed solutions, including ramping up new product introductions, the challenges will be hard to overcome quickly and will likely weigh on shares for some time, especially if its annual outlook is cut further as some expect.

"This print highlighted many long-term concerns around the stock," Morgan Stanley analyst Alex Straton said in a research note, pointing to intensifying competition and saturation in the U.S. market, among other risks. She suggested that Lululemon shares now deserve a valuation similar to a specialty apparel retailer, rather than its previous status as a high-growth apparel brand.

Jefferies analyst Randal Konik said that the company's second consecutive cut to its annual outlook shows it has lost visibility into its business as it contends with volatile sales. It recorded its weakest month of the quarter in July, when most retailers saw their strongest sales, and its lackluster third-quarter outlook suggests a tough back-to-school shopping period, he said.

"When you see something like that, it means that while the brand's relevant, it's becoming less relevant," Konik said, noting that Lululemon was the "it" brand during the Covid-19 pandemic. Its casual offerings have since lost ground to new entrants like Alo Yoga and Vuori, he said.

Konik forecasts steeper guidance cuts through the end of the year and sales declines in 2026, noting the brand seems to be losing business to competitors at a faster clip.

Much of next year's performance will hinge on whether the company can spark demand with new casual styles, William Blair analyst Sharon Zackfia said, acknowledging its performance apparel continues to draw shoppers.

"Forty percent of the mix is non-performance apparel, so they need to figure out a way to get that into a better, firmer footing so that it's not dragging down the overall business," she said.

Signs of weakness in its once-strong China business and a bigger-than-expected tariff headwind only add further pressure, she said.

"All of those dynamics really create a lot less profitability in the near term, and probably a greater lack of clarity on how quickly trends rebound and when," she said. "I think that's kind of what the Street is really grappling with today."

Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com

data.title: Lululemon Could Face Further Guidance Cuts Ahead, Analysts Say
Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1237 ET - Strathcona Resources continues to buy up MEG Energy shares as it looks to position itself to oppose rival Cenovus' buyout of the Canadian oil sands producer. Strathcona said it had acquired another 6.03 million shares of MEG, which accepted a buyout by Cenovus last month. The new shares brings Strathcona's stake to 14.2% from 11.8%. Strathcona paid about C$172.7 for the latest batch. In May, Strathcona made an offer for MEG Energy, which the MEG board advised shareholders not to accept. MEG later accepted a C$7.9 billion buyout offer from Cenovus. Strathcona says it intends to vote its MEG shares against the resolution to approve the acquisition of MEG by Cenovus at a special meeting of MEG shareholders scheduled to be held on October 9, 2025. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1229 ET - Docusign sees the government as an opportunity for growth, CEO Allan Thygesen says in an interview with CNBC. The electronic-signature company recently signed an agreement with the General Services Administration to give government agencies a discount on Docusign products, Thygesen says. Docusign's work with federal agencies has been relatively small, but it presents a large opportunity, he says. The company is also trying to push more AI tools beyond just signatures, which help summarize and analyze agreements, and is aiming for larger customers. Shares up 5.3%. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1214 ET - Secondhand shopping has surged this year with consumers turning to thrift stores as tariffs cause price hikes in conventional apparel. But thrift stores were gaining even before tariffs, according to a report from Placer.ai. Visits to thrift stores, which rose 7.9% year-over-year in the second quarter, have risen 39.5% from the second quarter of 2019, while the broader clothing industry has seen only 9.5% growth over that span. Economic pressures and sustainability concerns have fueled that growth, which has extended beyond thrift store's traditional base to include more visits from rural and suburban consumers and higher-income demographics, the report says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1155 ET - Toll Brothers is dialing back some of its home starts to clear its accumulated inventory of spec houses in a dormant housing market, says Wedbush analyst Jay McCanless. Toll Brothers primarily builds higher-end, luxury homes and as a result may not be suffering from the competitive supply that is forcing builders to offer aggressive incentives in the entry-level homes market. Still, McCanless says Toll Brothers management is hoping to have two or fewer spec homes per community, which "should prevent any inventory buildup." Management is aiming for a mix of 40% affordable luxury homes, 40% move up/luxury and 20% active adult, McCanless says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1128 ET - Toll Brothers is seeing less of a sales slowdown than other homebuilders amid high mortgage rates and affordability concerns because it caters to a wealthier buyer with its average home price over $1 million. As a result, it's not as dependent on mortgage rate buy downs as other companies. But it still needs to offer some incentives to complete sales especially in its affordable luxury segment, which may continue to hurt its margins in the near-term, says Wedbush analyst Jay McCanless. "Toll is still offering design center and other incentives along with selected buy downs to spur traffic," McCanless says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1108 ET - Cracker Barrel has reverted to its old logo after pressure from customers and the White House — but that doesn't answer the question of where its million-dollar marketing strategy will head now, Benchmark analyst Todd Brooks says. The streamlined logo was part of the Southern comfort food restaurant's ongoing strategic transformation. Brooks poses the question of whether Cracker Barrel's choice not to go ahead with the logo change will have other ramifications for that transformation. Whatever it decides, the stakes are high, Brooks says: Cracker Barrel is spending $600 million to $700 million in capital expenditures on the transformation effort over three years. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1054 ET - Restaurant foot traffic appears to have tapered off in August compared with July, Benchmark analyst Todd Brooks says. Benchmark's panel of 31 restaurants had an average year-over-year traffic slow down of 175 basis points in August versus July, Brooks says. This trend is likely setting restaurant companies up for a tough 3Q season. About 40% of the restaurants in Benchmark's panel are on track to miss Wall Street estimates for same-store sales in the current quarter. Meanwhile, 13% of the restaurants are on track to beat with same-store sales by 150 basis points or more. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1029 ET - Tight credit spreads and strong performance in the European credit market have prompted issuance of asset-backed securities (ABS) in new jurisdictions such as Poland and Norway, S&P Global Ratings' Arnaud Checconi says in a report. Asset-backed securities are investments backed by income-producing assets such as loans and leases. "European ABS issuance volumes for 2025 are on track to exceed those in 2024," Checconi adds. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1022 ET - Investors are broadly predicting the death of software due to AI, but the industry's struggles will eventually subside, say JPMorgan analysts. The relative valuation of software against semiconductors is approaching a level that has historically yielded strong alpha for software stocks, the analysts say, adding that software stocks typically perform better in rate-cutting environments, which will likely take shape in coming weeks. The analysts also say HubSpot offered a strong response to the AI-induced-pressure narrative this week by saying "we provide the customer context to drive insights about growth--that is not there in the LLM." In addition, JPMorgan says HubSpot now offers a hybrid pricing model, rather than purely seat-based, offering a flexible way for customers to pay. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1020 ET - Airbus's commercial aircraft deliveries in August bring encouraging news for the European plane maker during a typically slow month, RBC Capital Markets analysts write in a research note. The group delivered 61 aircraft to customers last month, up 30% year-on-year. While the company's 434 deliveries so far this year are down 3% from the same period last year, analysts say the strong August figures suggest the group will be able to meet its 2025 target of about 820 aircraft deliveries. The fourth quarter has since 2010 accounted for nearly 33% of Airbus's annual deliveries, the analysts note. If the trend persists, they believe the company should be able to hit its delivery target. Airbus shares trade 1.5% lower at 184.08 euros. (william.gray@wsj.com)

1019 ET - Many long-time homeowners are sitting on substantial equity gains, but today's shifting market means selling successfully requires more strategy and preparation than in years past, Realtor.com says. Starting the process early--before the peak spring season--can make the difference between a smooth sale and missed opportunities. Nearly half--45.2%--of homeowners have lived in their homes for more than 15 years, with one in four staying put for more than 25 years. U.S. home prices have tripled from $145,000 in June 2000 to $435,300 in June 2025, according to the National Association of Realtors. For many owners, that means hundreds of thousands of dollars in potential equity. A typical homeowner who bought in 2005 has seen their home value rise by about 90%, boosting equity by more than $200,000. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1003 ET - Investors have expressed concern that Google AI Overviews will hurt HubSpot by taking away valuable web traffic. But the company anticipated this change years in advance and pivoted beyond search to alternate emerging channels including YouTube and podcasts, say JPMorgan analysts. HubSpot is now cited in more large language models than any other customer relationship management company and is now tracking when AI generates leads for customers' businesses, which will help the company sustain demand in the AI era. "HubSpot's adept maneuvering should be viewed as a feather in its cap and an ability to see around corners," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Energy Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1237 ET - Strathcona Resources continues to buy up MEG Energy shares as it looks to position itself to oppose rival Cenovus' buyout of the Canadian oil sands producer. Strathcona said it had acquired another 6.03 million shares of MEG, which accepted a buyout by Cenovus last month. The new shares brings Strathcona's stake to 14.2% from 11.8%. Strathcona paid about C$172.7 for the latest batch. In May, Strathcona made an offer for MEG Energy, which the MEG board advised shareholders not to accept. MEG later accepted a C$7.9 billion buyout offer from Cenovus. Strathcona says it intends to vote its MEG shares against the resolution to approve the acquisition of MEG by Cenovus at a special meeting of MEG shareholders scheduled to be held on October 9, 2025. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0946 ET - Oil prices extend losses in afternoon trade, with Brent crude down 1.7% to $65.83 and WTI falling 1.9% to $62.28 a barrel. The benchmarks are on track for a weekly drop of around 2.5%, pressured by concerns over a looming supply glut as key OPEC+ members prepare to gather online Sunday and an unexpected rise in U.S. stockpiles last week. David Oxley, chief commodities economist at Capital Economics, says the firm expects OPEC+ to take a pause before raising output further from the second quarter of next year. Still, he noted that with prices having found some support over the summer, the group might feel it has room to bring more supply back to market sooner. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0934 ET - Crude futures are down for a third session and on track for weekly losses as the market watches for the weekend's OPEC+ meeting and digests the unexpected 2.4 million barrel build in U.S. crude stocks reported by the EIA. "Technical momentum appears to have completely reversed and is now heading lower," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. Increased OPEC+ barrels should keep the crude oil market well supplied, and the group could decide on bringing even more barrels online in October, he says. "With expectations of a surplus ahead, it is hard to get overly bullish."WTI is down 1.7% at $62.38 a barrel and Brent is off 1.6% at $65.93. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0924 ET - U.S. natural gas futures extend their slow grind higher, but with ample storage heading into the low-demand shoulder season keeping a limit on the rally. Focus remains on the production outlook as demand is likely to be a bit below normal in coming weeks, Gary Cunningham of Tradition Energy says in a note. Gas for January 2026 is testing resistance at $4.26, he adds, "but still has some upside potential as physical buyers who missed out on the retraction below $4 step into the market for their winter hedges." The Nymex front month is up 1% at $3.104/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0549 ET - The European natural-gas prices rise in early trade, as warm weather boosts demand for LNG and union strikes in France disrupt nuclear power generation. The benchmark Dutch TTF contract is up 0.8% to 32.69 euros a megawatt hour and on track for a weekly gain of 3.4%. High temperatures in parts of Japan have driven up electricity demand and led to fuel stockpile drawdowns, according to analysts at ANZ. Meanwhile, industrial action at France's top power producer, Electricite de France, curbed as much as 2 gigawatt of output. "France's power exports continued and operations at French LNG import terminals were not affected," the analysts say. "However, the market remains sensitive to any risks of supply disruptions or sudden jumps in demand." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0413 ET - Orsted's downgrade to earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization is a small negative ahead of Friday's vote on a proposed 60 billion Danish kroner rights issue, Jefferies analyst Ahmed Farman writes. The company lowered its full-year Ebitda guidance to between 24 billion and 27 billion kroner, from 25 billion-28 billion kroner previously, driven by lower-than-normal offshore wind speeds during July and August. Orsted also mentioned it expects a construction delay at its Greater Changhua 2b project, which will also adversely affect Ebitda in 2025. Jefferies expects details on the rights issue in the coming days. Shares rise 0.9%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0402 ET - Oil prices are set for a weekly loss of more than 1% for the first time in three weeks amid concerns over OPEC+ supply and an unexpected build in U.S. crude stockpiles. In early trade, Brent crude is down 0.5% to $66.67 a barrel, while WTI falls 0.5% to $63.13 a barrel. OPEC+'s online meeting on Sunday will be closely watched by traders, as further production increases would exacerbate concerns over a looming supply glut. Meanwhile, a bearish U.S. inventory report further weighed on sentiment. The latest EIA data showed U.S. crude stocks rose by 2.4 million barrels last week--against expectations of a 2-million-barrel fall--and are now at their highest level since early August, according to ANZ. Further price declines are though capped by risks of disruption to Russian supplies from Ukrainian attacks on key energy infrastructure and the spectrum of tougher U.S. sanctions. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0308 ET - Orsted's guidance downgrade is a negative ahead of the rights issue prospectus which is expected early next week, RBC analyst Alexander Wheeler writes. Following lower-than-normal offshore wind speeds, Orsted lowered its 2025 Ebitda guidance to 24 billion-27 billion Danish kroner ($3.7 billon to $4.2 billion), from 25 billion-28 billion kroner previously. Company consensus is at 26.5 billion kroner, Wheeler adds. Lower wind speeds have impacted Ebitda by 1.2 billion kroner, with a construction delay at the Greater Changhua 2b project providing a 300 million kroner hit. Additionally, commissioning of Greater Changhua 2b has been delayed from late 2025 to the third quarter of 2026 due to damage to an export cable, he adds. Shares fall 1.7%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: S.Africa's electricity regulator backs ArcelorMittal's bid for discounted tariffs
data.description:
Key points:
  • Regulator says it does not dictate electricity charges
  • Steelmaker must re-apply for favourable terms with Eskom
  • ArcelorMittal closing down plants partly due to high power costs

ArcelorMittal South Africa meets the criteria for discounted electricity tariffs in terms of set rules, the country's energy regulator said on Friday, backing a plea by the loss-making steelmaker.

The regulator, however, said it did not have the authority to dictate an electricity tariff and ArcelorMittal would have to negotiate favourable terms with state-owned power utility Eskom.

ArcelorMittal is winding down its money-losing South African long steel operations, which have been weighed down by high costs, including electricity expenses, while battling weak demand and an influx of imports.

The steelmaker petitioned the National Energy Regulator of South Africa (NERSA) to review a decision by Eskom to reject its application for discounted tariffs.

Eskom argued that ArcelorMittal did not meet the criteria for negotiated pricing agreements (NPAs), which are typically offered to energy-intensive industrial power users.

"NERSA considered the eligibility criteria and other factors

outlined in the NPA framework and concluded that ArcelorMittal South Africa substantially complied with the prescribed

criteria," the regulator said in a statement.

ArcelorMittal said this week it was winding down its long steel plants at Newcastle and Vereeniging as planned on September 30, after failing to secure government concessions on utility charges and import duties, among other demands.

The closure of the plants could result in the loss of more than 3,500 direct jobs, the company said.

ArcelorMittal said the state-owned Industrial Development Corporation, an 8% shareholder in ArcelorMittal South Africa, was conducting due diligence on the steelmaker.

data.title: REG - HSBC Holdings PLC - Transaction in Own Shares
data.description:
RNS Number : 2849Y HSBC Holdings PLC 05 September 2025  

HSBC HOLDINGS PLC

5 September 2025

Transaction in own shares

HSBC Holdings plc ("HSBC" or the "Company") announces that it has purchased for cancellation the following number of its ordinary shares of US$0.50 from Merrill Lynch International ("Merrill Lynch") as part of its buy-back announced on 31 July 2025.

UK Venues

5 September 2025

2,248,042

£9.7176

All repurchases on the London Stock Exchange, Aquis Exchange, Cboe Europe Limited (through the BXE and CXE order books) and/or Turquoise ("UK Venues") are implemented as "on Exchange" transactions (as such term is defined in the rules of the London Stock Exchange) and as "market purchases" for the purposes of the Companies Act 2006.

Hong Kong Stock Exchange

Date of purchase:

5 September 2025

1,213,200

Highest price paid per share:

HK$101.9000

Lowest price paid per share:

HK$100.4000

HK$100.9836

All repurchases on The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Limited ("Hong Kong Stock Exchange") are "off market" for the purposes of the Companies Act 2006 but are transactions which occur "on Exchange" for the purposes of the Rules Governing the Listing of Securities on The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Limited and which constitute an "on-market share buy-back" for the purposes of the Codes on Takeovers and Mergers and Share Buy-backs.

Since the commencement of the buy-back announced on 31 July 2025, the Company has repurchased 100,997,163 ordinary shares for a total consideration of approximately US$1,284.8m.

Following the cancellation of the shares repurchased on the UK Venues, the Company's issued ordinary share capital will consist of 17,320,251,709 ordinary shares with voting rights. There are no ordinary shares held in treasury. Cancellation of the shares repurchased on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange takes longer than those repurchased on the UK Venues and a further announcement of total voting rights will be made once those shares have been cancelled.

The above figure of 17,320,251,709 may be used by shareholders as the denominator for the calculations by which they will determine if they are required to notify their interest in, or a change to their interest in, the Company under the Financial Conduct Authority's Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules.

In accordance with Article 5(1)(b) of the Market Abuse Regulation (EU) No 596/2014 (as it forms part of domestic law of the United Kingdom by virtue of the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018, as amended), a full breakdown of the individual trades made by Merrill Lynch on behalf of the Company is available via the link below.

data.title: SuperRare to Launch “Facade” by Gavin Meeler on September 10
data.description:
SuperRare is set to feature “Facade”, a new collection by artist Gavin Meeler, starting September 10. The drop includes three unique 1/1 pieces—On the Brink, Unraveled, and The Glare—each with a reserve price of 1.5 ETH. The expressive, abstract works will be available on the on-chain art marketplace for a limited bidding period.

RARE Info

SuperRare is a platform designed for the issuance, collection, and trading of unique digital art supported by non-fungible tokens (NFTs) on the Ethereum blockchain.

On SuperRare, artists can create their artworks directly from their own wallets, and every bid, sale, and collector is recorded and stored in the token's history on the blockchain. Using to Ethereum, the platform ensures the security and verification of authorship and ownership of the art.

SuperRare provides an opportunity for artists to sell their works directly to collectors, and collectors can acquire unique and exclusive digital art pieces. This opens up new possibilities for artists and collectors in the digital world.

data.title: China's Nvidia Breakup? The AI Chip Divorce Silicon Valley Feared Is Happening
data.description:
A fresh momentum shift is building in China's AI supply chainand it may spell long-term trouble for Nvidia . Alibaba is now testing domestic accelerators, Cambricon just posted blowout earnings, and upstart DeepSeek is already training models on Huawei hardware. Beijing, meanwhile, has shown little interest in letting Nvidia's H20 chips regain traction. The subtext? China's tech ecosystem could be moving past its reliance on imported silicon. What was once a policy talking point is now becoming a market reality.

To be clear, Nvidia still dominates the high end. The company's H20 shipments to China hit roughly 1 million units this yearpossibly front-loaded before further US restrictions. But on-the-ground, local firms are adapting fast. Analysts expect Cambricon to ship 143,000 chips in 2024, while Huawei could produce up to 200,000 advanced units in 2025. Companies are learning how to run models on legacy hardwareand doing it well enough to meet domestic demand. That may be good enough for President Xi's AI+ initiative, which prizes broad deployment over benchmark supremacy. Cambricon, however, issued a caution to investors, saying its stock had likely decoupled from its current fundamentals and warning of persistent supply-chain risks.

The big unknown is Washington. On its latest earnings call, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang floated a real possibility that the US could allow sales of its upcoming Blackwell chips into Chinaa move that would dramatically raise the stakes. These GPUs outclass both H20s and their domestic peers, and could tempt Chinese buyers back if made available. For now, though, the tone has shifted. Between sanctions, policy nudges, and emerging domestic alternatives, Nvidia's long-term hold on the China AI market looks less certain than ever.

data.title: Bond yields tumble as weak jobs report raises chances of a half-point Fed rate cut
data.description:
By Joy Wiltermuth

Longer-term Treasury yields look like a safety play for the first time since April's market shock

U.S. Treasurys look to be back in the flight-to-safety camp.

U.S. Treasury yields fell dramatically on Friday, as traders braced for the Federal Reserve to potentially cut interest rates more deeply than previously anticipated to shore up a dimming jobs market.

Friday's August jobs report reflected a four-month stalling streak in the labor market, including a surprising revision to June data that indicated the economy lost jobs that month.

"I don't think people were expecting to see that negative month," said George Catrambone, head of fixed income, Americas, at DWS - noting that the latest revisions to many job categories outside of healthcare showed negative growth for the first half of this year. "We haven't necessarily been pricing in that."

As a result, 2-year Treasury yields BX:TMUBMUSD02Y tumbled by about 12 basis points to 3.47%, at last check, while the all-important 10-year yield BX:TMUBMUSD10Y was 9 basis points lower at 4.07%, according to FactSet data.

"The real question now becomes how many rate cuts follow, and from our lens the persistent weakening in the labor market supports our ongoing call for a total of 75 basis points of rate reduction by year-end," Kathy Bostjancic, chief economist at Nationwide, said in emailed comments.

The odds of the Fed greenlighting a bigger rate cut of 50 basis points in September were close to 14%, according to the CME FedWatch Tool, but still 86% for a regular cut of 25 basis points.

Steve Englander, Standard Chartered Bank's global FX strategist, earlier this week laid out a case for why a jumbo rate cut this month may be justified, arguing that markets and policymakers have been misreading labor data.

To be sure, next week's inflation data could shake up the narrative again, but for now it looks like stagflation risks - or elevated inflation combined with a wobbling economy - aren't a primary concern for investors.

With the labor market "flatlining," risks that could create a potential recession were back in focus, said Jeffrey Schulze, head of economic and market strategy at ClearBridge Investments. That's changed the tone from the initial morning reaction to drag on stocks, he noted, with investors starting to price in slower growth and a less robust earnings backdrop than was initially anticipated.

Treasury debt back in style

That backdrop, with the Fed potentially needing to protect the economy with rate cuts, was putting a fresh shine on the $29 trillion Treasury market.

Investors for much of this year tended to hit the sell button in tandem on U.S. stocks and Treasurys, particularly in longer-duration 10-year notes and 30-year bonds BX:TMUBMUSD30Y during pockets of turmoil. That's raised concerns that U.S. assets could be losing their luster.

Investors instead have been favoring gold (GC00) and other traditional flight-to-safety assets except for the dollar DXY, which was lower Friday and down 10% this year.

Yet with fiscal concerns driving global government bond yields higher across developed markets, U.S. Treasurys may once again look like "one of the cleanest of some dirty shirts in the laundry pile," said Catrambone at DWS.

The fresh appeal of U.S. Treasurys on Friday had the 2-year yield on pace for a new low this year, while the 10-year rate was moving closer to its sub-4% low set in the wake of President Trump's April tariff shock.

That could reflect a move by investors to lock in yields while they still can. There's still an estimated $7.3 trillion parked in U.S. money-market funds, a shorter-term cash surrogate. Those investors could face reinvestment risks down the road if yields rapidly dwindle as the Fed cuts rates.

Earlier this week, a jump in bond yields helped pressure stocks lower. The S&P 500 SPX and the Nasdaq Composite COMP on Friday briefly roared to fresh intraday records - but all three major U.S. stock indexes, including the Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA, have since turned negative.

Schulze at ClearBridge said he think this is just a soft patch for stocks and "a buy-the-dip" opportunity. He thinks stocks can move higher in 2026 as stimulus from Trump's signature tax and spending bill kicks in, as the Fed cuts rates and as there's more clarity on the tariff front.

See: U.S. unemployment rate jumps to nearly 4-year high. Weak 22,000 increase in August jobs report tees up Fed rate cut.

-Joy Wiltermuth

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Black Unemployment Rises Again in Jobs Report — WSJ
data.description:
By Konrad Putzier

The racial gap in the labor market is widening. The unemployment rate for Black Americans rose to 7.5% in August, the Bureau of Labor Statistics said Friday. That's up from a seasonally adjusted 7.2% in July and 6.0% in May.

Meanwhile, the white unemployment remained unchanged at a seasonally adjusted 3.7% in August and was slightly lower than in May.

Economists say that rising unemployment among Black Americans is a potential warning sign for the broader labor market. Black workers are more likely to hold low-skilled or entry-level jobs, which are often cut first when the economy weakens. The share of Black Americans who are unemployed is at the highest level since October 2021.

The Hispanic or Latino unemployment rate rose to 5.3% in August from 5.0% in July, although it declined slightly on a year-over-year basis.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Global oil and gas company layoffs in 2024 and 2025
data.description:
International oil and gas companies plan to shed more jobs in 2025 after cutting thousands last year as prices remain under pressure and consolidation gathers pace.

Benchmark Brent crude futures are down about 10% year-to-date, impacted by increased OPEC+ output and persistent demand uncertainty tied to U.S. trade policy.

A snapshot of energy companies that have announced job cuts in 2024 and 2025:

The oilfield services provider has been cutting staff in recent weeks, according to two sources familiar with the matter.

data.title: Brownmine Launches Appli+AI to Democratize AI Technology for Small and Midsize Businesses
data.description:
Atlanta, Georgia--(Newsfile Corp. - September 5, 2025) - Brownmine officially launched Appli+AI on September 2, 2025, introducing a comprehensive AI automation, consulting, and education service specifically designed for small and midsize businesses (SMBs) and underconnected communities. The new platform addresses a critical market gap where advanced AI technology has remained largely inaccessible to smaller organizations with limited resources.

Appli+AI delivers four core value propositions to bridge the technological divide. The service focuses on productivity enhancement through task automation, scalable systems that grow with businesses, measurable ROI through efficiency and revenue increases, and practical education that demystifies AI technology for everyday business applications.

Segment-Specific Solutions for Diverse Business Needs

The platform offers tailored solutions across multiple business categories to maximize practical impact. Local businesses can implement AI agents for 24/7 customer service, potentially boosting service quality without additional staff costs. Marketing automation and inventory management systems streamline operations and may improve customer engagement

Nonprofits and community leaders gain access to AI-powered data analysis for potentially better resource allocation and impact measurement. Grant writing assistance and volunteer management tools aim to help maximize organizational effectiveness.

Entrepreneurs potentially benefit from streamlined marketing campaigns, automated lead generation, and AI-powered business planning tools. These solutions reduce operational overhead while aiming to accelerate growth potential.

Professional service providers across industries can leverage AI for everyday productivity enhancement, including automated scheduling, proposal drafting, and client communication management. These applications deliver immediate value while requiring minimal technical expertise from users.

Challenging Traditional AI Implementation Models

The launch represents a significant departure from the current AI implementation landscape where technical expertise and substantial financial resources have been prerequisites for adoption. Appli+AI positions advanced AI technology as an equalizer that enables smaller organizations to compete effectively with larger enterprises.

Traditional AI deployment has favored corporations with dedicated IT departments and substantial budgets. Appli+AI eliminates these barriers by providing turnkey solutions that require no specialized technical knowledge or significant upfront investment from users.

"Every business, every entrepreneur, and every community deserves the chance to innovate, scale, and thrive with AI," said Mike A. Williams, Founder and CEO of Brownmine. "AI should not be reserved for big corporations. Appli+AI exists to make sure no one is left behind in this technological revolution."

Broader Economic Impact and Innovation Acceleration

Beyond immediate business applications, the Appli+AI launch signals a fundamental shift in how AI technology may be distributed across the economic spectrum. By making advanced tools accessible to previously underserved segments, the initiative could accelerate innovation in sectors that have been left behind in the AI revolution.

The service creates new models for technology democratization that prioritize practical application over theoretical concepts. This approach focuses on delivering tangible business value through automation tools that integrate seamlessly into existing workflows and operations.

Small businesses and community organizations can now access the same caliber of AI technology that has previously driven competitive advantages for major corporations. This technological leveling represents a potential catalyst for widespread economic transformation across multiple sectors.

About Brownmine and Appli+AI

Brownmine developed Appli+AI as a comprehensive solution for organizations seeking to harness AI technology without the traditional barriers of technical complexity and resource requirements. The service combines automation tools, strategic consulting, and practical education to deliver measurable business outcomes.

SMBs, entrepreneurs, and community leaders can explore resources, training, and tailored AI solutions through our websites. The service emphasizes accessibility and practical application while maintaining the sophisticated capabilities typically associated with enterprise-level AI implementations.

For more information about Appli+AI and its tech solutions for small and midsize businesses, visit www.appliai.ai or ww.brownmine.com to contact our team for additional details and demonstration opportunities.

Media Contact:

Charlene Allen

Operations and Client Manager

Email: charlene@brownmine.com

To view the source version of this press release, please visit https://www.newsfilecorp.com/release/264951

data.title: Toronto Stocks Inch Higher, Enghouse Systems Fall on 3Q Profit, Revenue Declines
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Toronto-listed stocks edged forward.

Sector performance was mixed Friday. Primary losses in energy, tech services, and commercial services offset gains in materials, tech and health services stocks.

Canada's S&P/TSX Composite Index rose by 0.2% to 28972.10 and the blue-chip S&P/TSX 60 was virtually unchanged at 1715.55.

On the employment front, Canada's unemployment rate rose more than expected in August, hitting 7.1%, its highest since May 2016 outside of the height of the pandemic. According to Statistics Canada, the economy lost a net 65,500 jobs last month, on top of 40,800 lost the month before. Market expectations were for employment to rise by 10,000.

Shares in Enghouse Systems fell by 8.8% to 20.82 Canadian dollars ($15.06) after reporting lower profit and revenue in the fiscal third quarter as the technology company grapples with macroeconomic uncertainties.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.title: Tesla’s Musk pay scheme mixes two kinds of madness
data.description:
The carmaker’s board wants to gift $1 trln to its boss with a new incentive plan. He has to hit goals that start from the weak benchmarks of past promises but rise to aptly far-fetched targets, like $8.5 trln in market value. The trade-off: speeding Musk’s path to control.

Full view will be published shortly.

Follow Jonathan Guilford on X and Linkedin.

CONTEXT NEWS

Electric-car maker Tesla disclosed in a securities filing on September 5 a new proposed incentive award scheme for Chief Executive Elon Musk. Under the plan, Musk would receive up to 12 tranches of restricted stock awards, up to nearly 424 million at most, representing a 12% stake.

To unlock each tranche, Tesla must hit one market capitalization milestone and one operational milestone. Awards begin at a market value of $2 trillion with the final tranche achieved if the market value reaches at least $8.5 trillion by 2035.

data.title: Arabica Futures Slip as Tariff Uncertainty Loom
data.description:
Arabica coffee futures fell toward $3.85 per pound on Friday as traders awaited clarity on whether US President Donald Trump’s proposed 50% tariff on imports from top supplier Brazil would be enforced.

Prices have been volatile, climbing to three-month highs in August before easing in early September.

The market remains underpinned by supply concerns, speculative buying, and dwindling certified stocks, with the tariff threat adding further pressure.

Brazil’s 2025/26 Arabica crop is forecast at 35.2 million bags, down 11.2% from the previous harvest, though overall production is expected to edge higher on improved productivity.

Weather risks—including drought, irregular rainfall, and frost—are adding to uncertainty, while global inventories sit at historically low levels.

With limited certified stocks and constrained availability from alternative origins, short-term supply challenges continue to support prices.

data.title: CAC 40 Index Ends the Week 0.38% Lower at 7674.78 — Data Talk
data.description:
The CAC 40 Index is down 29.12 points or 0.38% this week to 7674.78

  • Down for two consecutive weeks
  • Down 294.91 points or 3.70% over the last two weeks
  • Largest two week point and percentage decline since the week ending April 11, 2025
  • Today it is down 24.14 points or 0.31%
  • Down for two consecutive trading days
  • Down 44.93 points or 0.58% over the last two trading days
  • Largest two day point and percentage decline since Tuesday, Sept. 2, 2025
  • Down three of the past four trading days
  • Off 6.86% from its record close of 8239.99 hit Wednesday, May 15, 2024
  • Off 6.48% from its 52-week high of 8206.56 hit Tuesday, Feb. 18, 2025
  • Up 11.83% from its 52-week low of 6863.02 hit Wednesday, April 9, 2025
  • Rose 4.39% from 52 weeks ago
  • Off 6.48% from its 2025 closing high of 8206.56 hit Tuesday, Feb. 18, 2025
  • Up 11.83% from its 2025 closing low of 6863.02 hit Wednesday, April 9, 2025
  • Year-to-date it is up 294.04 points or 3.98%

Source: Dow Jones Market Data, FactSet

data.title: REG - Lloyds Banking Group - Transaction in Own Shares
data.description:
RNS Number : 2896Y Lloyds Banking Group PLC 05 September 2025  

05 September 2025

TRANSACTIONS IN OWN SECURITIES

Lloyds Banking Group plc (the "Company") announces today that it has purchased the following number of its ordinary shares, from Morgan Stanley & Co. International plc (the "Broker").

Ordinary Shares

Date of purchases: 05 September 2025

Number of ordinary shares purchased: 9,391,973

Highest price paid per share (pence): 80.7200

Lowest price paid per share (pence): 79.2400

Volume weighted average price paid per share (pence): 79.7999

Such purchases form part of the Company's existing share buyback programme and were effected pursuant to the instructions issued to the Broker by the Company on 20 February 2025, as announced on 21 February 2025.

The Company intends to cancel these shares.

In accordance with Article 5(1)(b) of Regulation (EU) No 596/2014 (the Market Abuse Regulation) (as such legislation forms part of assimilated law as defined in the EU (Withdrawal) Act 2018), a full breakdown of the individual trades made by the Broker on behalf of the Company as part of the buyback programme is set out in the Schedule to this announcement available through the link below:

http://www.rns-pdf.londonstockexchange.com/rns/2896Y_1-2025-9-5.pdf

- END -

For further information:

data.title: Apple's $9B Power Move in India Changes Everything
data.description:
Apple just quietly posted its strongest year yet in India, with annual revenue climbing to nearly $9 billion through Marchup 13% from the $8 billion it made the year before. That growth, while still a blip in Apple's global pie, signals something bigger: India is no longer a slow-burn optionality story. It's turning into a real engine. iPhones were the key driver once again, helped by credit-card partnerships, trade-in deals, and a barrage of student discounts designed to offset India's steep import taxes. According to a person familiar with the numbers, demand for MacBooks also rose sharply, hinting at a broader upshift in Apple's brand equity across the country's rising middle class.

The company's moves on the ground back up the numbers. In just the past week, Apple added two new flagship stores in Bangalore and Pune, with Noida and Mumbai next on deck. That retail rollout follows its 2023 decision to carve India into a standalone sales regiona structural move that reflects how Apple's senior team now sees the market. As of now, iPhones command about 7% market share locally, but management appears to be playing the long game. Apple devices in India carry higher price tags than in the USan entry-level iPhone 16 costs nearly $907 there versus $799 statesidebut CEO Tim Cook has repeatedly called India one of Apple's fastest-growing geographies.

Behind the scenes, India is also becoming a second factory floor. One out of every five iPhones is now made in the country, and Apple is actively scaling up capacity across five plants, including two that just came online. That shift has been years in the makingbut with Chinese production facing renewed scrutiny, it's now more relevant than ever. Cook reportedly plans to use India as a key node in the company's US-bound supply chain, in part to reduce dependence on China. Taken together, Apple's retail expansion, surging sales, and supply chain localization suggest that India may be stepping into the role China once played: not just a growth market, but a strategic hedge.
